title: GB Paralympic cyclist Schrager handed two-year ban after positive drug test
dek: Daphne Schrager, a Paris 2024 silver medallist in Paralympic cycling, has received a two-year ban following a positive test for the banned substance Ligandrol. The National Anti-Doping Panel rejected her claims of environmental contamination, though finding no intentional use.
author: The GoCheckMySports Desk
key_fact: Schrager's two-year ban is backdated to 2 June 2025 and expires 1 June 2027, leaving her eligible to compete at the 2028 Los Angeles Paralympics but ineligible for the current Commonwealth Games in Glasgow.
bottom_line: Watch for whether Schrager pursues an appeal of the NADP decision and what procedural options may still be available to her. The ruling underscores how anti-doping enforcement holds athletes to a strict liability standard even absent proof of intentional wrongdoing.
body: Daphne Schrager, 25, a silver medallist from the Paris 2024 Paralympics, has been handed a two-year ban for testing positive for the banned substance Ligandrol following an out-of-competition test conducted last year, according to BBC Sport.

Ligandrol is a selective androgen receptor modulator that selectively builds muscle and bone mass. The National Anti-Doping Panel (NADP) tribunal, per BBC Sport, found that while Schrager had not intentionally used a banned substance, she had not proved that it was inadvertent environmental contamination. Schrager had argued she had been exposed to environmental contamination, pointing to two possible scenarios: exposure at a gym where she received a massage, or at a shared home address.

The ban is backdated to 2 June 2025, when Schrager was initially suspended, and will end 1 June 2027. As a result, she will be ineligible to compete for England at the Commonwealth Games currently taking place in Glasgow. However, the timing of the ban's expiration means she remains eligible to compete at the 2028 Paralympic Games in Los Angeles, per the reporting.

Schrager has maintained her innocence, insisting she has never cheated. The NADP tribunal's decision draws a distinction between intent and liability, ruling against her contamination defence without finding deliberate rule violation.
